SUMMARY:
A number of techniques have been applied to the characterization of the singly dispersed atoms on SACs, including STM, HAADF-STEM, EDX, EELS, XAS, FTIR, SERS,

Techniques for the characterization of single atom catalysts
============================================================

Ping Qi[†](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#fn1 "Select to navigate to footnote")a, Jian Wang[†](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#fn1 "Select to navigate to footnote")a, Xavier Djitcheu a, Dehua He[![Image 5: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002 "Select to open ORCID record for Dehua He (orcid.org/0000-0003-0027-7002) in a new window")b, Huimin Liu [![Image 6: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263 "Select to open ORCID record for Huimin Liu (orcid.org/0000-0001-8258-8263) in a new window")*a and Qijian Zhang [![Image 7: ORCID logo](https://pubs.rsc.org/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X "Select to open ORCID record for Qijian Zhang (orcid.org/0000-0003-1992-031X) in a new window")*a

a School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning University of Technology, Jinzhou 121001, P. R. China

b Innovative Catalysis Program, Key Lab of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

Received 22nd October 2021 , Accepted 17th December 2021

First published on 5th January 2022

* * *

Abstract
--------

Single atom catalysts (SACs) are a hot research area recently. Over most of the SACs, the singly dispersed atoms are the active sites, which contribute to the catalytic activities significantly compared with a catalyst with continuously packed active sites. It is essential to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Several techniques have been applied for the characterization of the dispersion states of the active sites over SACs, such as Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), etc. In this review, the techniques for the identification of the singly dispersed sites over SACs are introduced, the advantages and limitations of each technique are pointed out, and the future research directions have been discussed. It is hoped that this review will be helpful for a more comprehensive understanding of the characterization and detection methods involved in SACs, and stimulate and promote the further development of this emerging research field.

* * *

[![Image 8: image file: d1ra07799f-p1.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p1.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")

[![Image 9: image file: d1ra07799f-p2.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p2.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")

After graduation, he continued to study for a master's degree in materials and chemical engineering at the Liaoning University of Technology, and his research interest is the photo-thermal synergistic catalytic conversion of CO 2 to methanol._

[![Image 10: image file: d1ra07799f-p3.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p3.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")

Now she is working in Liaoning University of Technology as a professor. Her research interests are photochemistry, environmental chemistry and heterogeneous catalyst design._

[![Image 11: image file: d1ra07799f-p4.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p4.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-p4_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")

His research interests are nano catalysis and energy chemistry, which is dimethyl ether reforming to hydrogen, application of nano Ni based catalyst in CH 4 reforming, photo-thermal catalysis, etc._

* * *

1 Introduction
--------------

With the application of nanocatalysts in various reactions and melioration of synthesis methods, catalysts of small particle sizes have been prepared.[1–4](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit1 "Select to navigate to reference") It is widely recognized that catalytic activity increases with the reduction of particle size because more active atoms are exposed. Enlightened by this trend, investigators will expect to obtain high atom utilization from high dispersion catalysts in which each active site could be exposed to participate in chemical reactions. The concept of “single-atom catalysis” was firstly introduced by Zhang et al. in 2011. In their work, a highly efficient and robust Pt 1/FeO x catalyst was synthesized for CO oxidation reaction by the co-precipitation method. Besides, they demonstrated why Pt single atom catalysts (SACs) showed particularly excellent properties with low loading of Pt.[5](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit5 "Select to navigate to references")
Inspired by this remarkable research, SACs have been widely investigated in various catalytic reactions. It has been found that SACs have several advantages.[6–8](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit6 "Select to navigate to reference") (1) In SACs, the utilization of active center atoms is greatly improved due to their high dispersion.[9–12](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit9 "Select to navigate to reference") (2) The strong interaction at the active center–support interface of SACs tunes the electronic properties, leading to the tailorable catalytic activity.[13–16](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit13 "Select to navigate to reference") (3) The highly dispersed atoms on the surface of the catalyst have limited numbers of configurations to adsorb the reactant molecules. Compared with traditional nanoparticle (NP) catalysts, side reactions could be significantly inhibited and the selectivity towards the desired products could be improved over SACs.[17](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit17 "Select to navigate to references")

Because of these advantages, SACs are widely applied in reactions where a single active site works, such as CO oxidation reaction,[18–20](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit18 "Select to navigate to reference") hydrogenation reactions[21–23](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit21 "Select to navigate to reference") and so on. On the contrary, SACs cannot catalyze reactions that require surface ensemble sites,[24,25](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit24 "Select to navigate to reference") such as hydrocarbon (C ≥ 2) oxidation reaction[26](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit26 "Select to navigate to references") and hydrogenolysis reaction,[27](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit27 "Select to navigate to references") in which traditional nanoparticle catalysts have been extensively explored. Consequently, SACs are a promising research direction in specific reactions.

In single atom catalysis, it is particularly important to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Up to now, several techniques have been utilized to investigate the presence of single atoms (SAs) in SACs. These techniques could be roughly divided into two types. One type includes high resolution scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) and high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscope (HAADF-STEM), which can directly observe the atomic morphology.[28](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit28 "Select to navigate to references") The other type consists of X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), which can provide auxiliary evidence for the existence of SAs (the information of SACs that can be obtained by different techniques are listed in [Table 1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#tab1 "Select to navigate to table")). Giving an introduction of these techniques to the readers will give them a clear picture and inspire more studies on the area of SACs. As some of the techniques, such as STM, HAADF-STEM, XAS, FTIR and XPS have already been reviewed somewhere,[28](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit28 "Select to navigate to references") this review emphasizes on the applications of EDX, EELS and SERS in SACs. We will briefly introduce the principles of these techniques, illuminate the advantages and limitations of each technique, and emphasize the necessity of combining multiple characterization approaches to fully investigate SACs. In addition, ex situ techniques can not give the precise information of SAs on the catalyst surface in reaction atmosphere, because the atomic structure of SACs might undergo transformation. Hence, in situ/operando techniques are also introduced in this review for the determination of the singly dispersed sites over SACs under the working conditions.

**Table 1**The obtained information about SACs and merits/disadvantages of different technologies

| Techniques | Uses | Information obtained | Merits/disadvantages | Typical resolution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| XAS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Valence state; electronic structure; coordination number | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; it is sensitive to chemical groups, local structures and spatial coordination; the test time is short and the damage to the sample is small; complex data processing | 0.1 nm |
| STM | Ex situ and in situ operando | Morphology structure; atomic distribution state | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; the working environment has universal applicability; the result is easily affected by environment | 0.01 nm vertical resolution |
| HAADF-STEM | Ex situ and in situ operando | Atomic distribution state; morphology structure | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; thick and low contrast specimens can be observed; micro diffraction can be realized; imaging is limited to selected areas | 0.08 nm |
| EDX | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element type analysis | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; used with HAADF-STEM; low energy resolution | 0.1 nm |
| EELS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element and chemical state analysis | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; high electronic structure sensitivity; insensitive to high number atoms | 0.1 nm |
| SERS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Crystal structure; chemical bonding | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; difficult sample preparation process; only specific elements could be analyzed | 0.1 nm |
| FTIR | Ex situ and in situ operando | Molecular groups; chemical bonding | Atomic level sensitivity; in situ recognition of adsorption process; only specific molecules can be detected | 0.1 nm |
| XPS | Ex situ and in situ operando | Element analysis; chemical state | Atomic level resolution; it can detect detailed electronic structure and geometric information; in situ detection process; the information is not persuasive | 0.1 nm |

* * *

2 Characterization techniques for evidence of single atoms in SACs
------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2.1 X-ray absorption spectroscopy

XAS is a special technique, which has been widely applied to determine the geometric chemistry and electronic structure of catalysts. XAS could provide the strongest evidence for the presence of SAs. When the irradiated energy of the X-rays is greater than the energy required to excite a specific element on a catalyst, the absorption coefficient is dramatically increased. The absorption edge affords information on the atomic structure of the catalyst. The excessive energy is emitted as an electromagnetic wave, which could be scattered back by adjacent atoms with the outgoing of electromagnetic wave, causing oscillations in the detective signal. XAS contains two parts. (1) The X-ray absorption near edge structures (XANES) and (2) the extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS). XANES could reflect the electron and oxidation states while EXAFS could provide information on chemical bonding, the interatomic distance and the coordination number of targets element.[29–33](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit29 "Select to navigate to reference")
XAS was universally utilized to reflect the dispersion of active sites on the surface of SACs.[34](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit34 "Select to navigate to references") For example, Chen et al. synthesized a SAs Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 catalyst and used XAS to determine the electronic and geometric structures of the singly dispersed Au sites.[34](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit34 "Select to navigate to references") The drastic difference between the XANES features for Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 and Au foil, as well as Au NPs/mpg-C 3 N 4. The result in [Fig. 1a](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure") implied that the Au atoms were possibly singly dispersed on support, the same threshold value (11![Image 12: [thin space (1/6-em)]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_2009.gif)919.4 eV) for Au over Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 and AuCl indicated that the valence of Au on Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 was +1. The lacking the peak assigned to the Au–Au bond in the r-space spectrum of Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 further confirmed the singly dispersed state of Au on Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 ([Fig. 1b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure")).[35](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit35 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 13: image file: d1ra07799f-f1.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f1.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 1**(a) XANES spectra of Au L 3 edge. (b) r-space spectra of Au 1/mpg-C 3 N 4 and a series of reference samples. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [35](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Wiley-VCH, copyright 2018.

Atomic Cu 1-N 2/HCNS catalyst was prepared by the simple reduction deposition-acid leaching method. Due to the unique atomic structure of Cu in the catalyst, the activity of SAs Cu-N 2 is much better than that of Cu-N 3. Under the same reaction condition, the catalytic activity of the former was increased by 3.4 times. Cu 1-N 2/HCNS catalyst afforded higher selectivity and stability than Cu NPs or nanoclusters.[36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit36 "Select to navigate to references")

In order to further investigate the electronic properties and coordination environment of Cu 1-N 2/HCNS, XANES and EXAFS were utilized. The results in [Fig. 2a](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure") indicated outline of Cu K-edge XANES. Cu 1-N 2/HCNS exhibited an energy absorption edge between Cu+ and Cu 2+ standards in the range of E 0 (about 8979 eV). The corresponding oxidation state of Cu is +1.5 by linear combination fitting. On the EXAFS spectrum ([Fig. 2b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig2 "Select to navigate to figure")) of Cu 1-N 2/HCNS, there was only a peak at 1.9 Å, which revealed the dispersion of single Cu atoms in the whole HCNs matrix.[36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit36 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 14: image file: d1ra07799f-f2.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f2.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 2**(a) Cu K-edge XANES spectra. (b) Cu K-edge EXAFS spectra. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [36](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit36 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2019.

In addition to the examples listed above, XAS could be applied in situ to reveal the structure of SACs under realistic reaction conditions.[37–41](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit37 "Select to navigate to reference") For instance, Wei et al. used in situ XAS to track the evolution of the catalytic active sites of Co SACs under alkaline hydrogen evolution reaction.[38](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit38 "Select to navigate to references") They revealed that the unsaturated singly dispersed Co sites was subjected to undergo a structural and valence state evolution under the reaction atmosphere. They would transform into an HO–Co 1–N 2 moiety upon the adsorption of a hydroxyl group and further involve into H 2 O–(HO–Co 1–N 2) by adsorbing water molecules.[38](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit38 "Select to navigate to references")

The center atoms with different coordination numbers or ligands exhibit different XANES adsorption curves. Consequently, it is the most straightforward and beneficial approach to identify the existence of SAs. XAS and in situ XAS are critical in providing the structural details of SACs for unraveling the reaction mechanisms at an atomic level. Yet, the operation of XAS is quite complex and the analysis/interpretation of XAS data is complicated, which limits the reproducibility of XAS final results.

### 2.2 Scanning tunneling microscopy

The tunneling effect applies for STM. When the distance between the catalyst surface and the needle-like tip is less than 1 nm, the electronic clouds of catalyst surface and the needle-like tip overlap. If a voltage is introduced between the catalyst and the needle-like tip, tunneling current is generated. The tunneling current is exponentially related to the distance between the needle-like tip and the catalyst surface. When the lengthwise height is slightly changed, the intensity of tunneling current is greatly modified to realize the ultra-high atomic resolution (the principle of STM is depicted in [Fig. 3](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig3 "Select to navigate to figure")). The lateral resolution of STM is 0.1 nm, which makes it applicable to investigate the singly dispersed atoms in SACs.[42–45](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit42 "Select to navigate to reference")

[![Image 15: image file: d1ra07799f-f3.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f3.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 3**Schematic diagram of STM. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [46](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit46 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2012.

For example, Flytzani-Stephanopoulos et al. utilized high-resolution STM to characterize the distribution of Pt atoms on Cu(111) surface. The results in [Fig. 4a and b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig4 "Select to navigate to figure") indicated that the Pt atoms are singly and randomly distributed throughout the surface of Cu(111), with most of the Pt atoms on the terraces and near the step edges of Cu(111).[47](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit47 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 16: image file: d1ra07799f-f4.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f4.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f4_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 4**STM and high resolution STM images of Pt 1/Cu(111). This figure has been reproduced from ref. [47](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit47 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018.

STM could be upgraded into in situ STM by introducing reactants and/or heat into the characterization system. In situ STM allows the direct observation of the SACs structure under the reaction atmosphere. For instance, in situ STM is applied by Sykes et al. to investigate the structure of Pt 1/Cu 2 O in CO oxidation reaction at low temperatures. The experimental and simulated STM images ([Fig. 5a and b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig5 "Select to navigate to figure")) demonstrated that CO was adsorbed onto Pt SAs.[48](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit48 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 17: image file: d1ra07799f-f5.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f5.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f5_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 5**(a) and (b) Experimental and simulated in situ STM images of Pt 1/Cu 2 O in CO oxidation reaction. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [48](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit48 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Nature Publishing Group, copyright 2018.

STM has been applied to investigate the singly dispersed atoms on the surface of SACs. STM could be manipulated under many working conditions, including ultra-high vacuum, gaseous atmosphere, and liquid atmosphere, at a temperature range of 0–1273 K.[49](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit49 "Select to navigate to references") The vertical resolution of STM is 0.1 nm. However, it could not be used to recognize the embedded atoms.

### 2.3 High angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy

HAADF-STEM is an approach to observe catalyst morphologies. The principle of HAADF-STEM is depicted in [Fig. 6](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig6 "Select to navigate to figure"). When electrons are accelerated by a high voltage to attack the catalyst surface, different interactions occur between the incident electrons and atoms. Elastically scattered electrons are distributed over wide scattering angles above the catalyst because electrons can not penetrate the catalyst, while non-elastic scattering electrons (transmission electrons) are distributed across small angles. If only electrons of wide range angles are detected, dark-field imaging are obtained.

[![Image 18: image file: d1ra07799f-f6.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f6.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f6_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 6**Schematic illustration of HAADF-STEM. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [50](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit50 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Wiley-VCH, copyright 2021.

The bright spots in the image always reflect the real atoms. More electrons are diffracted by elements of higher atomic numbers, which then appears brighter in the HADDF-STEM imaging. HADDF-STEM could be utilized to investigate catalyst properties.[50](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit50 "Select to navigate to references")

Regarding SACs, if there is a great discrepancy between the atomic number of the singly dispersed atom and support,[51–56](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit51 "Select to navigate to reference") singly dispersed atoms could be distinguished by HAADF-STEM. The X-rays produced by electronic attacking of catalyst surface are collected by EDX detector and electrons passing through the catalyst are collected by the EELS detector. Multiple devices are synergistically utilized to explore the atomic states of SAs. The details of EDX and EELS will be minutely described later.

Fe SACs were synthesized by pyrolyzing coordinated polymer strategy. [Fig. 7](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig7 "Select to navigate to figure") displayed the dispersion of Fe SAs in Fe-C/Al 2 O 3 catalyst. The HAADF-STEM image straightforwardly demonstrated that Fe existed in the state of SAs. Compared with Fe nanoparticle catalyst, Fe SAC has noteworthy advantages. Fe SAC possesses not only the improved atomic utilization efficiency but also stronger interaction between the active sites Fe and matrix Al 2 O 3. The strong interaction leads to (1) the change of the chemical state of Fe atoms and (2) the enhanced adsorption capacity for reactant gases. In addition, the limited number of configurations between the active sites Fe and reactant gases inhibits the occurrence of side reactions.[57](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit57 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 19: image file: d1ra07799f-f7.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f7.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f7_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")
**Fig. 7**HAADF-STEM image of Fe-C/Al 2 O 3. This figure has been reproduced from ref. [57](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit57 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier, copyright 2021.

In situ HAADF-STEM is an approach to characterize the dispersion state of the active sites and visualize the dynamics of SACs under reaction atmosphere.[41,57–63](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit41 "Select to navigate to reference") For instance, Li et al. visualized the transformation of noble metal particles (Pd, Pt, and Au) into singly dispersed atoms at a temperature above 900 °C in an inert atmosphere via in situ HAADF-STEM ([Fig. 8a and b](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#imgfig8 "Select to navigate to figure") are the images for Pt NPs before and after introducing heat and inert atmosphere into the HAADF-STEM chamber).[64](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlehtml/2022/ra/d1ra07799f#cit64 "Select to navigate to references")

[![Image 20: image file: d1ra07799f-f8.tif](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f8.gif)](https://pubs.rsc.org/image/article/2022/RA/d1ra07799f/d1ra07799f-f8_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window")

### RSC Advances

## Techniques for the characterization of single atom catalysts

![Check for updates](https://crossmark-cdn.crossref.org/widget/v2.0/logos/CROSSMARK_Color_horizontal.svg)
![ORCID logo](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)
![ORCID logo](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)
![ORCID logo](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)

\* 

Corresponding authors

a

School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning University of Technology, Jinzhou 121001, P. R. China

b

Innovative Catalysis Program, Key Lab of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

### Abstract

Single atom catalysts (SACs) are a hot research area recently. Over most of the SACs, the singly dispersed atoms are the active sites, which contribute to the catalytic activities significantly compared with a catalyst with continuously packed active sites. It is essential to determine whether SACs have been successfully synthesized. Several techniques have been applied for the characterization of the dispersion states of the active sites over SACs, such as Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS), *etc.* In this review, the techniques for the identification of the singly dispersed sites over SACs are introduced, the advantages and limitations of each technique are pointed out, and the future research directions have been discussed. It is hoped that this review will be helpful for a more comprehensive understanding of the characterization and detection methods involved in SACs, and stimulate and promote the further development of this emerging research field.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) analyzes elemental composition and distribution in single-atom catalysts, providing insights into atom types and their arrangement.
Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) offers detailed information about electronic structure and chemical states, crucial for understanding the behavior of single atoms in SACs.
Extended X-ray Absorption Fine Structure (EXAFS) characterizes coordination number and interatomic distances, essential for elucidating the local structure around active sites in SACs.
X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) provides chemical state and electronic structure information, vital for understanding the interactions between atoms in single-atom catalysts.
Characterization techniques enhance our understanding of active sites and their interactions, paving the way for the design of more efficient single-atom catalysts.
